narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 66.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that shaped this outcome. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar—suggesting your later undergraduate performance raised concerns about sustaining the rigor of law school and bar prep. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile also at 1, far below the typical 6 for successful bar takers, indicating early academic challenges that the model sees as a persistent risk factor. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.60 is actually stronger than the 3.24 average among those who passed—pulling the prediction somewhat toward success—it wasn't enough to offset those lower decile rankings. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 31 compared to the 37 average for passers, your lower-middle family income bracket (less common at 9.4% among successful students versus middle or higher for most), and your full-time study plans (which align with 93% of passers), played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical admitted students, who often show higher decile rankings and stronger overall test scores, your profile appeared riskier for bar success in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many with similar starting points have succeeded through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and focus on building a stronger academic record in any upcoming coursework to improve those decile standings. You might also explore programs with bar passage support or connect with mentors from similar family backgrounds who've navigated this path. With strategic steps, you can reshape your profile for future applications and prove the model wrong.
